name:
Data Structures and Algorithms
Prerequisites 	INTE 11223 - Programming Concepts
INTE 12213 - Object Oriented Programming
Co-requisites 	None
Credit Value 	3
Hourly Breakdown
(Notional Hrs.)
Theory 	Practical 	Guided Self-study
45 	30 	75
Course Aim/Intended Learning Outcomes:
After completing this module, the students should be able to,
1. Identify the properties of a good algorithm and a good program
2. Determine and analyse the efficiency of often used algorithms
3. Design correct and efficient algorithms for solving real-world problems
4. Implement data structures often used in computer programming
5. Discuss the real-world applications of different data structures
6. Apply data structures for solving real-world problem
7. Design and develop efficient computer programs using data structures and algorithms
Course Content:
Algorithms and programs, correctness and efficiency of an algorithm, space complexity, time complexity, the
best, average and worst cases of time complexity, linear, logarithmic, linear logarithmic, quadratic,
polynomial, exponential and factorial functions of time complexity, asymptotic notations, classical searching
and sorting algorithms, divide-and-conquer approach, recursion, atomic and composite data types,
fundamental data structures, advanced data structures, real-world applications.
Teaching /Learning Methods:
Lectures, supervised hands on practical sessions, and guided self-study assignments
Assessment Criteria:
End semester examination and continuous assessments
Continuous Assessment
0 – 25%
Final Assessment
75 – 100%
Details: quizzes, mid-term, assignments
0 – 25 %, 0 – 25 %, 0 – 25 %
Theory (%)
100%
Practical (%)
0%
Other (%)
(specify)
Recommended Reading:
1. Sartaj Sahni, (2004), Data Structures, Algorithms and Applications in C++, Silicon Press
2. Richard F. Gilberg and Behrouz A. Forouzan, (2005), Data Structures: A Pseudocode Approach with C++,
2nd edition, Course Technology
3. Steven S. Skiena, (2020), The Algorithm Design Manual, 3rd Edition, Springer-Verlag
4. Material provided in CAL.

Year of Study 	2
Semester 	2
Course Code 	INTE 22253
Course Name 	Distributed Systems and Cloud Computing

Warehouse Management and Industrial Shipping
Pre-Requisites 	None
Co-Requisites 	None
Credit Value 	3
Hourly Breakdown
(Notional Hrs.)
Theory 	Tutorial/ Practical 	Guided Self-study
45 	15 	90
Course Aim/Intended Learning Outcomes:
On completion of this course, the student should be able to:
1. Analyze the basic process of a warehouse operation
2. Analyze the KPIs related to warehouse operation
3. Evaluate warehouse operation models
4. Evaluate shipping options for industrial cargo
5. Analyze shipping markets and key indexes
6. Evaluate main operations related to industrial shipping process
Course Content:
Warehouse Process Management: Receiving, Put away, Storage, Order picking, Shipping, Distribution
Management, Network Design, Warehousing models, Warehouse activity profiling, Measuring and benchmarking
warehouse operations, Warehouse process pallet storage and retrieval systems, Store functions and
responsibilities, Store procedures and planning, Store System costs and rates, Materials - Handling considerations
and systems, Computerizing warehouse operations, Warehouse layout planning, Warehouse safety and security,
Best practices, Audits and compliance, Warehouse location selection, Inventory management
Shipping market and players, Liner shipping industry, Tramp shipping industry, Freight market, Baltic Freight Index
(BFI), Vessel new building market, Demolition market, Sale and purchase market, Liner agency services, Strategic
alliances, consortia, Merger and acquisitions, Shipping networks, Seaport operations, Route planning, Fleet
management, Port choice, Network design: Pendulum services, Round the world services, Ship owners, Insurance,
Charter parties, Shippers and consignees, Freight forwarders, Chartering types, Voyage, time and bareboat
chartering, Yard planning, Crew scheduling, Ship planning, Berth planning, Strategic, Port operating models,
Tactical and operational level issues
Teaching /Learning Methods:
Lectures, interactive classroom sessions and case analysis
Assessment Criteria:
End of course unit examination and continuous assessments
Continuous Assessment
40%
Final Assessment
60%

0 - 20 %, 0 – 20%

10%- 90%

Recommended Reading:
1. Edward, H F, (2004), World-Class Warehousing and material handling, TATA McGraw-Hill, New Delhi.
2. Rahul, V A, (2005), Supply Chain Management: Concepts and Cases, PHI Learning, New Delhi.
3. John, J C and Robert, A N, (2009), A Logistics Approach to Supply Chain Management, Cengage Learning.
4. Joel, D W, Keong, L and KeahChoon, T, (2012), Principle of Supply Chain Management – A Balanced Approach,
Cengage Learning.
5. Ronald, H B and Samir, K S, (2007), Business Logistics/ Supply Chain Management, Pearson Education.
6. Paul R M, Donald F W, (2008), Contemporary Logistics, PH of India.
7. Tompkins, JA, White, Y A, Bozer, EH and Frazer, (1996), Facilities Planning, Wiley.
8. Peter Brodie, P. (2018) Commercial Shipping Handbook.
9. Lun, Y.H.V., Lai. K-H. and Cheng. T.C.E. (2010) Shipping and Logistics Management. Springer Link.
10. Levinson, M. (2008) The Box: How the Shipping Container Made the World Smaller and the World Economy
Bigger.
11. Material provided in CAL.

Course Code 	MGTE 31433

Advanced Optimization Methods in Management Science

Sustainability and Economics in Logistics and Supply Chain
Prerequisites 	None
Co-requisites 	None
Credit Value 	3
Hourly Breakdown
(Notional Hrs.)
Theory 	Tutorial/ Practical 	Guided Self-study
45 	30 	75
Course Aim/Intended Learning Outcomes:
On completion of this course, the student should be able to:
1. Recognize global sustainability issues associated with supply chain and logistics systems
2. Discuss the approaches for economic, environmental, and social sustainability in logistics and supply chain
3. Analyse and discuss measures to mitigate negative impacts from logistics and supply chain
4. Identify KPIs for measuring sustainability aspects in logistics and supply chain
5. Develop sustainable solutions for logistics systems
6. Discuss economics implications in logistics and transport
7. Comprehend the impacts of transport economics and policy on other industries and markets
8. Discuss directions for transport policy, infrastructure development and related implications
Course Content:
Environmental, economic and social impacts of logistics, Techniques adopted for assessing the environmental
impacts, Sustainability related KPIs, Pollution estimation methods, Negative externalities, Strategies for
environmental sustainability, Equity issues in transport, Non-motorised transport, Park and Ride, Ride
Sharing, Mobility management, Health, safety and security issues in transport and logistics, Social exclusion,
Green supply chain management, Supply chain innovation, Sustainable development goals, Closed-loop
supply chain, Reverse logistics, Energy sources, Energy consumption, Emission calculation, Resource
allocation, Rules and regulations for environmental sustainability, Transport economy: Utility theory,
Opportunity cost, Elasticity of transport demand, Transport supply, Transport pricing and equity, Tax and
subsidy, Economic and diseconomy of scale, Generalized costs, Analysis on transport policy, Logistics and
transport market structures, Alliances, Transport infrastructure developments, Cost-benefit analysis,
Marginal cost and Fixed cost pricing, Discriminatory pricing, Methods of financing transport infrastructure and
investments, Investments risks, Ownership issues, Emission tax, Emission trading, Congestion pricing,
Benefits/cost analysis of transport projects
Teaching /Learning Methods:
Lectures, interactive classroom sessions and case analysis
Assessment Criteria:
End of course unit examination and continuous assessments

60%

1. Meng, L. and Bock, J.D. (2016) Sustainable Logistics and Supply Chains, Innovations and Integral
Approaches, Springer Link.
2. Grant, D.B., Trautrims, A. and Wong, C.Y (2015)Sustainable Logistics and Supply Chain Management
(Revised Edition), Kogan Page Publishers.

78
Year of Study 	4
Semester 	2
Course Code 	MGTE 42213
Course Name 	Industrial and Systems Engineering

Advanced Supply Chain and Logistics Applications
Prerequisites 	All compulsory modules for OSCM
Co-requisites 	None
Credit Value 	3
Compulsory/Optional 	Compulsory
Hourly Breakdown
(Notional Hrs.)
Theory 	Practical/Tutorial 	Guided Self-study
45 	15 	90
Course Aim/Intended Learning Outcomes:
After completing this module, the students should be able to,
1. Plan a logistics system with its components
2. Discuss integration of logistics functions
3. Evaluate factors, different methods and analysis techniques used for logistics system
4. Comprehend advanced concepts in Supply Chain Management
5. Formulate and solve real-life supply chain and logistics related models
6. Develop competency in logistics network planning and distribution networks
7. Rationalize different techniques for managing physical distribution
8. Develop and apply computer-based techniques of distribution planning and control
9. Understand emerging trends, national and international laws and practices in logistics and supply chain
Course Content:
Logistics system, Logistics activity profiles, Short, medium, and long term planning for logistics infrastructure,
Demand planning, Scheduling, Forecasting, Facility design and operations, Demand and order management,
Advanced Inventory models, Logistics systems integration, Reverse logistics systems, Outsourcing levels - Third
Party Logistics (3PL) Provider / Lead logistics Provider (LLP) (Linking to Logistics Ecosystems, 2PL, 4PL, 5PL, Drop
shipping, Last Mile Logistics), Supply chain restructuring, Decision levels in a supply chain, Tripple A supply chain,
Freight forwarding, Decision making for logistics systems, Derived demand in transport, Transport supply,
Infrastructure planning, Design and layouts of infrastructure, Investments in transport infrastructure, Maritime,
Aviation, Land transport, Transport Demand and Supply, Formulation of mathematical models for solving issues
in logistics systems, Aggregate and disaggregate planning, Facility location problems, Logistics network planning,
Green Supply Chain, Closed-loop supply chain, Introduction to Physical Distribution: Interfaces & environment;
functional areas; Systems design & measurement; Decision making; Distribution planning and scheduling,
Organization and administrative costing; Components of physical distribution systems, Distribution
Requirements Planning (DRP)systems, analysis and benefits of DRP; Distribution networks, Emerging trends in
logistics, National and international law in logistics, legislation, regulations, safety requirements, and
professional standards
Teaching /Learning Methods:
Lectures and case discussions
Assessment Criteria:
End of course unit examination and continuous assessments.
Continuous Assessment
25 - 50%
Final Assessment
50 - 75%
